coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot-functionalized upconversion nanoparticles for highly sensitive detection of food contaminant azodicarbonamide
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jialin
Yang, Yaqing
Aryee, Aaron Albert
Wang, Ningning
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: Excessive consumption of flour products containing excessive azodicarbonamide (ADA) has been associated with some adverse effects in humans. Thus, developing a facile method to monitor the amount of ADA in flour is extremely significant. Herein, a graphene quantum dot (GQD) sensitized upconversion nanoparticles (GQD-UCNPs) was designed to monitor the amount of ADA in flour with high specificity and sensitivity. Surface coating of UCNPs with GQD by coordination interaction significantly enhances the upconversion luminescence (UCL) intensity and the water solubility of UCNPs. Copper ions (Cu2+) can quench the UCL intensity of GQD-UCNPs through electron transfer processes. After introduction of glutathione (GSH), the UCL intensity of GQD-UCNPs gradually increases because of the interaction between GSH and Cu2+, accompanied by the generation of Cu+. The sulfydryl group of GSH can be oxidized to disulfide bond by ADA, which hinders the interaction between GSH and Cu2+, preventing the recovery of UCL intensity. Thus, the presence of ADA decreases the UCL intensity of GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ + GSH system, allowing for the quantitative detection of ADA. The nanosensor realized a highly sensitive and specific ADA detection with a high detection limit down to 0.055 μM. Furthermore, excellent recovery rates in spiked real samples were obtained, indicating that the designed method possesses good prospects for practical application. Notably, this method broadens the application prospects of upconversion technology in the field of food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Upconversion nanoparticles
Upconversion luminescence
Azodicarbonamide
Food safety

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The analysis of ADA in flour
Synthesis and characterization of GQD, OA-UCNPs and GQD-UCNPs
Sensitivity and specificity of GSH and ADA detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (No. 232300421021 and No. 242300421342), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22004111 and No. 22274143), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2020M682327), Henan Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 202002009), the Tackle Key Problems in Science and Technology Project of Henan Province (No. 222102310386), Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Henan Province (No. 22TRTSTHN002).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characteriation of GQD, OA-UCNPs and GQD-UCNPs. (a) TEM image of GQD. Inset: HRTEM of GQD. (b) TEM image of OA-UCNPs. Inset: HRTEM of OA-UCNPs. (c) TEM image of GQD-UCNPs. Inset: HRTEM of GQD-UCNPs. (d) The scanning TEM (STEM) element mapping of GQD-UCNPs. Scale bar: 25 nm.

The optical property of GQD-UCNPs. (a) UCL spectra of UCNPs and GQD-UCNPs (λex = 980 nm). (b) Lifetime spectra of UCNPs and GQD-UCNPs at 542 nm. (c) UCL spectra of GQD-UCNPs, GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+, GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ + GSH and GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ + GSH + ADA (λex = 980 nm). (d) Lifetime spectra of GQD-UCNPs, GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ and GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ + GSH at 542 nm.
The effect of reaction conditions on the UCL intensity recovery rate (I–I0)/I0, (a) Cu2+ concentration, (b) pH value, (c) temperature, (d) reaction time.

Sensitivity and specificity of GSH detection. (a) UCL spectrum of the GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ system with various concentrations of GSH excited at 980 nm (0–50 μM). (b) Linear relationship between GSH concentration and the UCL intensity ratio value of (I–I0)/I0. (c) The selectivity of GQD-UCNPs system for GSH detection. (d) UCL intensity of GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ + GSH system with various coexisting species (50 μM for GSH; 500 μM for L-Ser, L-Try, L-Arg, L-Lys, L-Leu, L-His, L-Asp, D-Glu, D-Tyr, L-Cys, glucose, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+ and blank). The condition of all above assay: [Cu2+] = 60 μM, and pH = 3.5.

Sensitivity and specificity of ADA detection. (a) UCL spectrum of the GQD-UCNPs system with various concentrations of ADA excited at 980 nm. (b) Linear relationship between ADA concentration and the ratio value of (I0–I)/I0. (c) The selectivity of GQD-UCNPs system for ADA detection. (d) UCL intensity of GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ + GSH + ADA system with various coexisting species (20 μM for ADA; 200 μM for L-Ser, L-Try, L-Arg, L-Lys, L-Leu, L-His, L-Asp, D-Glu, D-Tyr, L-Cys, glucose, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+ and blank). The condition of all above assay: [Cu2+] = 60 μM, [GSH] = 50 μM, and pH = 3.5.
Schematic illustration of the GQD-UCNPs nanosensor for ADA detection in flour.
Sample detection results of GQD-UCNPs system.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot-functionalized upconversion nanoparticles for highly sensitive detection of food contaminant azodicarbonamide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Food Laboratory of Zhongyuan, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Food Laboratory of Zhongyuan, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Jialin Wang and Yaqing Yang contributed equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive consumption of flour products containing excessive azodicarbonamide (ADA) has been associated with some adverse effects in humans. Thus, developing a facile method to monitor the amount of ADA in flour is extremely significant. Herein, a graphene quantum dot (GQD) sensitized upconversion nanoparticles (GQD-UCNPs) was designed to monitor the amount of ADA in flour with high specificity and sensitivity. Surface coating of UCNPs with GQD by coordination interaction significantly enhances the upconversion luminescence (UCL) intensity and the water solubility of UCNPs. Copper ions (Cu2+) can quench the UCL intensity of GQD-UCNPs through electron transfer processes. After introduction of glutathione (GSH), the UCL intensity of GQD-UCNPs gradually increases because of the interaction between GSH and Cu2+, accompanied by the generation of Cu+. The sulfydryl group of GSH can be oxidized to disulfide bond by ADA, which hinders the interaction between GSH and Cu2+, preventing the recovery of UCL intensity. Thus, the presence of ADA decreases the UCL intensity of GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ + GSH system, allowing for the quantitative detection of ADA. The nanosensor realized a highly sensitive and specific ADA detection with a high detection limit down to 0.055 μM. Furthermore, excellent recovery rates in spiked real samples were obtained, indicating that the designed method possesses good prospects for practical application. Notably, this method broadens the application prospects of upconversion technology in the field of food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety has attracted globally worldwide attention, especially as food production is expected to grow to 70% more by 2050(Yu et al., 2022). Food quality is closely related to human health. Hence, it is significantly imperative to detect the harmful substances and defuse the possible risks from production to consumption(P. L. Wang, Xie, et al., 2019). The abuse of food additives results in serious hazards to people's health(L. Guo et al., 2022). Flour takes up a large percentage in family diet. Owing to its excellent oxidation and bleaching performances, azodicarbonamide (ADA) often appears in flour as food additives(Ye et al., 2011). Under dry environment, ADA possesses high stability without acute toxicity. However, ADA can be decomposed to toxic semicarbazide and biurea during heat processing when it reacts with moist flour(L. Zhang et al., 2021). Thus, excessive consumption of flour products containing excessive ADA poses a great threat to humans. Hence, 45 mg/kg (ppm) is the maximum addition dose of ADA in flour in Japan, Australia and Singapore(L. Chen et al., 2016; Z. Chen et al., 2018). Therefore, developing a facile method to monitor the amount of ADA in flour is extremely significant.

To date, there are numerous methods including direct and indirect method applied for the detection of ADA. The direct method includes colorimetry(F. Chen et al., 2021; Z. Chen et al., 2018; Jeber et al., 2021; L. Zhang et al., 2021), near-infrared spectroscopy technique(Du et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2016; Osborne, 1983; X. Wang et al., 2018), Fourier transform infrared spetrometry (FTIR)(Otte et al., 1997), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)(Su et al., 2020) and capillary zone electrophoresis(L. Chen et al., 2016). The indirect method is to convert ADA to semicarbazide, and then the semicarbazide is detected by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays, electrochemical assays, and so on(Fang et al., 2013; Mulik et al., 2021; Szilagyi & de la Calle, 2006; Ye et al., 2011; Zhu et al., 2022). Although those detection methods demonstrate adequate accuracy and high efficiency, complex preprocessing and time-consuming detection process impose limitations on ADA detection. Therefore, constructing a simple, sensitive, and selective detection strategy for ADA is imperative.

Fluorescence detection possesses excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, and easy operation. There are numerous fluorescence probes such as quantum dots, carbon dots, organic dyes for the detection of hazardous substances in food. However, some of these have severe photobleaching and high background resulting in short duration of detection and high interference, which limit their application in food field. Upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) excited with near-infrared (NIR) light attract more and more attention in food due to their advantages of low background, weak photobleaching, large anti-stokes shift and high photochemical stability(Y. Guo et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2016; Peltomaa et al., 2021; N. Wang et al., 2022; Y. Xu et al., 2021; Y. Yang et al., 2023; M. Zhang, Wang, & Li, 2022). Cheng group(Z. Xu et al., 2023) designed a probe UCNP/Fe3O4@PCN-224 to detect cadmium ions in food, which effectively solved the background interference in samples. Chen group(Y. Zhang, Wang, & Li, 2022) designed and synthesized a probe that assembled Eu3+ exposed UCNPs with negative complex of magnetic nanoparticles and aptamer by electrostatic adsorption principle for the detection of tetracycline. However, UCNPs have the shortcomings of low water solubility, poor luminous efficiency and non-recognition group, which limit the application of UCNPs in the field of food quality monitoring. Graphene quantum dot (GQD) possesses puny sizes, strong edge effects and quantum confinement(Xuan et al., 2017). Furthermore, the excellent properties of GQD such as low toxicity, high chemical stability, good water solubility and biocompatibility enhance its application prospects in a several fields(Ghaffarkhah et al., 2022; Jia et al., 2022). There are numerous carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of GQD, so that the GQD has the advantages of excellent water solubility and functionalization(Chung et al., 2021). In addition, GQD can enhance upconversion luminescence of UCNPs by energy transfer due to their ability of absorbing and emitting NIR light(Laurenti et al., 2016). Thus, combining GQD with UCNPs is expected to solve the problems of low luminescence efficiency, poor water solubility, and difficulty in functionalization associated with traditional UCNPs, achieving sensitization and modification of UCNPs.

Herein, we have designed GQD-sensitized upconversion nanoparticles (GQD-UCNPs) with enhanced upconversion luminescence (UCL) and water solubility which exhibits a high specificity and sensitivity for ADA detection in flour (Scheme 1 ). This strategy selects NIR-excited UCNPs as luminescent nanomaterials, which is beneficial to eliminate the interference of background luminescence, reduce photobleaching, and improve the sensitivity of probe. To enhance the UCL intensity and water solubility of traditional UCNPs for constructing high sensitivity detection assays, we employed a two-step strategy. First, nitrosyl tetrafluoroborate (NOBF4) was modified on the surface of UCNPs by the ligand exchange reaction, obtaining water-soluble nitrosyl tetrafluoroborate-capped UCNPs (NOBF4-UCNPs). Then, surface coating of UCNPs with GQD was conducted through coordination interactions between imidazole groups of GQD and surface lanthanide ions of UCNPs, obtaining GQD-sensitized upconversion nanoparticles with enhanced UCL and water solubility. Concurrently, abundant amino groups distributed on the surface of the GQD-UCNPs could bind with copper ions (Cu2+). The UCL intensity of GQD-UCNPs can be quenched by Cu2+ through electron transfer processes. However, compared with amino group, the sulfhydryl group of glutathione (GSH) has a higher binding affinity for Cu2+(P. Wang, Xie, et al., 2019; F. Yang et al., 2021). After introduction of GSH, the UCL intensity of GQD-UCNPs gradually increased because of the interaction between GSH and Cu2+, accompanied by the generation of Cu+. ADA as an oxidant could oxidize the sulfhydryl group in the moist flour into a disulfide bridge and convert itself into biurea(Noonan et al., 2008; Ye et al., 2011). After introduction of ADA, the sulfhydryl group of GSH can be oxidized to disulfide bond by ADA, which hinders the interaction between GSH and Cu2+, preventing the recovery of UCL intensity. Thus, the UCL intensity of GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ + GSH system decreases with the increasing of ADA concentration (termed as [ADA]), allowing for the quantitative detection of ADA. Notably, it was successfully used to detect ADA in flour with good reliability and practicability. This work offers a convenient and sensitive detection strategy for hazardous substance, which is beneficial to expand the application of UCNPs in the field of food safety.

GQD was synthesized by a thermal pyrolysis. Briefly, 1 mmol citric acid (CA), 1.2 mmol histidine (His), 0.01 mmol octadecylamine and 3 mmol carbamide were completely dispersed in water and placed into an autoclave reactor at 180 °C for 5 h. Then, the crude product was treated with 1–3.5 kDa dialysis bag after being cooled to room temperature. The water was changed every 6 h. Finally, the GQD was obtained by freeze-drying method.

The NOBF4-UCNPs were synthesized via a ligand exchange reaction(Zhao et al., 2019). The oleic acid-coated UCNPs (OA-UCNPs) (NaYF4: 20%Yb, 0.8%Tm, 1.2%Er) were synthesized by a thermal decomposition method (the detailed steps were shown in Supporting Information). In general, 10 mg OA-UCNPs, 3 mL cyclohexane, 3 mL dimethylacetamide (DMF) and 5.8 mg NOBF4 were mixed in a centrifuge tube. After reacting 10 min using a vortex oscillator, the solution was caged for 5 min. The supernatant cyclohexane was dislodged and 3 mL isopropyl alcohol was added into the system. Finally, the product was collected by centrifugation (15,000 rpm, 15 min), washed three times with DMF and dispersed into DMF. The obtained NOBF4-UCNPs were stored at 4 °C.

The GQD-UCNPs were synthesized by ligand exchange method. Brifely, 0.1 mg GQD were mixed with 1 mg NOBF4-UCNPs in water. After stirring vigorously for 2 h, the system was purified by centrifugation. Eventually, the obtained GQD-UCNPs were dispersed in water (2 mg/mL) and stored at 4 °C.

0.02 mg/mL GQD-UCNPs, 60 μM Cu2+ ions and various concentrations of GSH (0–50 μM) were successfully mixed into a buffer system at pH 4.5 and incubated at 37 °C for 15 min. Then, the upconversion emission spectrum was collected under the excitation laser at 980 nm.

Different concentrations of ADA (0–20 μM), 50 μM GSH, 0.02 mg/mL GQD-UCNPs and 60 μM Cu2+ ions were added to a buffer system at pH 4.5 in turn and incubated at 37 °C for 15 min. Subsequently, the upconversion emission spectrum was collected under the excitation laser at 980 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analysis of ADA in flour
ce:section.ce:para: All the flour used in the experiments was purchased from market in Zhengzhou. Flour was treated according to the previous experimental methods (Z. Chen et al., 2018). Specifically, 10 mL acetone and 0.1 g flour were added into a centrifuge tube and sonicated for 20 min. To remove the insoluble substances, the system was treated by centrifugation at a rotate speed of 5000 rpm. 5 mL supernatant was extracted and the solvent evaporated using a rotary evaporator. Then, 5 mL water was added to disperse the crude product and the solution was purified by a 0.22 μM filter to eliminate residual insoluble substances. Finally, the ADA in flour was detected by the proposed method above. Simultaneously, the extract solutions of flour samples were added with various concentrations of ADA (0 μM, 0.1 μM, 0.5 μM and 0.8 μM), the recovery rates of the ADA in flour were obtained with the same detection method. According to the following equation: Recovery rate = (A-B)/C × 100% (A represents the measured concentration after spiking the standard concentration of ADA in samples, B represents the measured concentration of ADA in samples, and C represents the spiked concentration of ADA), the recovery data was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of GQD, OA-UCNPs and GQD-UCNPs
ce:section.ce:para: First, GQD was synthesized by simple pyrolysis of octadecylamine, His, CA and carbamide. To make graphene sheets, CA was selected owing to its special molecule structure. To achieve the coordination between GQD and UCNPs, His was selected for introducing imidazole groups in the edge of graphene sheets. Carbamide was used as the nitrogen source for providing amino groups in the edge of graphene sheets, which has strong binding ability with Cu2+. The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image confirmed the successful synthesis of GQD (Fig. 1 a). Sphere-structured GQD was observed with a uniform size of ∼2.1 nm. In addition, high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of GQD showed that the lattice spacing of GQD was about 0.22 nm (insert in Fig. 1a), which corresponded to the (100) facet of graphene. Moreover, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was investigated to identify the crystal structures of GQD. The XRD spectrum of GQD exhibited a specific peak at 26°, which was consistent with graphene (Fig. S1). A thermal decomposition method was selected to synthesize OA-UCNPs. As shown in Fig. 1b, the OA-UCNPs exhibited good dispersion and a diameter of ∼32.3 nm in cyclohexane. The lattice spacing of the OA-UCNPs was around 0.52 nm (insert in Fig. 1b), which was consistent with the (100) facet of the hexagonal crystal structure of NaYF4. Additionally, the characteristic peaks at 17.2°, 30.1°, 30.8°, 34.8°, 39.7°, 43.5°, 46.6°, 52.0°, 53.3°, 53.7°, 55.2°, 61.1°, 62.4°, 63.7° and 65.2° shown in the XRD spectrum of OA-UCNPs could be ascribed to the diffraction planes of the (100), (110), (101), (200), (111), (201), (210), (002), (300), (211), (102), (112), (220), (202) and (310) facet of the hexagonal β-UCNPs, respectively (Fig. S2).

Next, transferring hydrophobic OA-UCNPs to hydrophilic NOBF4 modified UCNPs and followed by GQD modification were conducted. Via a ligand exchange reaction, NOBF4 was modified on the surface of UCNPs. Then, via the coordination interactions between imidazole groups of GQD and surface lanthanide ions of UCNPs, GQD-UCNPs were constructed by modifying the obtained GQD on the surface of UCNPs. The GQD-UCNPs possessed excellent water dispersibility and had a uniform size of ∼34.1 nm (Fig. 1c). In addition, HRTEM revealed that the GQD-UCNPs had the lattice fringes of 0.22 and 0.52 nm which could be attributed to GQD and UCNPs (insert in Fig. 1c). Zeta-potential of the NOBF4 modified UCNPs (NOBF4-UCNPs) was determined to be +16.9 mV, and noticeably negative-shifted in contrast to that of the OA-UCNPs (+32.9 mV) (Fig. S3). After modification with GQD (−8.8 mV), the zeta-potential of GQD-UCNPs changed to +6.6 mV. To further confirm the successful assembly of the GQD-UCNPs, the scanning TEM element mapping analysis was carried out. Fig. 1d displayed that Er, F, Yb, and Y of UCNPs as well as N of GQD were present in GQD-UCNPs. From the FTIR analysis as illustrated in Fig. S4, a new peak at 1084 cm−1 contributed by BF4 − anions emerged in the FTIR spectrum of NOBF4-UCNPs after ligand exchange. Additionally, the characteristics peaks of –COO– (1562 and 1461 cm−1) and –CH2- (2927 and 2855 cm−1) belonging to the OA weakened, confirming the removal of OA. In the FTIR spectrum of GQD, the peak around 3300 cm−1 - 3600 cm−1 was ascribed to N–H and O–H symmetric stretching vibration, demonstrating the existence of –NH2 and –OH. The peak at 1628 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of –C=N bond in the imidazole rings. Notably, FTIR spectrum of GQD-UCNPs contains the characteristic IR absorption peaks of GQD. Together, all these data demonstrated that the GQD-UCNPs have been successfully prepared.

The optical property of GQD-UCNPs was further investigated. The UCL intensity of GQD-UCNPs enhanced significantly than that of NOBF4-UCNPs (Fig. 2 a). In the UCL spectrum, the emission at 522 and 542 nm are assigned to the transitions from 2H11/2 to 4I15/2 and from 4S3/2 to 4I15/2, respectively. In addition, the UCL spectrum of the GQD-UCNPs is extremely similar to that of UCNPs without GQD treatment, suggesting that the modification of GQD does not change the energy level of the UCNPs. The absorption spectrum of GQD was investigated to study the enhancement mechanism. A wide absorption peak at near infrared region appears in absorption spectrum of GQD, confirming that the GQD has the capacity of absorbing NIR excitation light (Fig. S5). The absorbed NIR light by GQD can transfer into the quanta to the UCNPs and improve the upconversion luminescent efficiency of UCNPs. Additionally, the coordination of GQD on UCNPs leads to an increase in the lifetime of green emissions, suggesting the effective energy transfer between GQD and UCNPs (Fig. 2b). These studies further proved that the enhancement of GQD-UCNPs is principally ascribed to the antenna ability of GQD absorbing NIR excitation light as well as the effective energy transfer between GQD and UCNPs (Scheme S1). Notably, GQD-UCNPs showed excellent optical stability even after a long-term storage, which further promotes its application prospects (Fig. S6).

To verify the feasibility of the GQD-UCNPs system for the detection of GSH and ADA, the upconversion luminescence spectra of different samples were investigated. In the absence of Cu2+ ions, GSH, ADA and ADA + GSH basically had little influence on the UCL intensity of the GQD-UCNPs (Fig. S7). But, the UCL intensity of GQD-UCNPs was effectively quenched after the addition of Cu2+ ions (Fig. 2c). Notably, the quenched luminescence was recovered after adding GSH into the solution system. However, the UCL intensity of GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ system was quenched again in the presence of GSH and ADA. All the above experimental results proved that our nanosensor possesses good feasibility for the detection of GSH and ADA. Next, the mechanism for Cu2+ quenching GQD-UCNPs was studied. The absorption of Cu2+ ions is non-overlapping with the emission of the GQD-UCNPs, which means that the dominant quenching mechanism is not based on inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Fig. S8). The luminescence lifetime of GQD-UCNPs was reduced from 142.93 μs to 129.91 μs after adding Cu2+ (Fig. 2d). This observation could be ascribed to the electron transfer from GQD-UCNPs to Cu2+. Compared with amino group, the sulfhydryl group of glutathione (GSH) has a higher binding affinity for Cu2+. After adding GSH, the luminescence lifetime basically recovered to 139.04 μs, illustrating that there was a strong interaction between Cu2+ and GSH. Moreover, Cu2+ ions were incapable of quenching the luminescence of the UCNPs without GQD (Fig. S9), evidencing that the GQD-UCNPs can combine with Cu2+ ions and close to the distance between Cu2+ ions and UCNPs. Based on above experimental results and previous studies(Ding et al., 2023; Liang et al., 2016; P. Wang, Xie, et al., 2019), it can be speculated that the main reason for the quenching mechanism might be the energy transfer from Yb3+ to Cu2+, which impedes the energy transfer from Yb3+ to Er3+ (Scheme S2). To further understand the sensing mechanism, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra was conducted (Fig. S10a). Compared with GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ system, GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ + GSH system exhibited a new shoulder peak around 300 nm, suggesting that there was an interaction between GSH and Cu2+ during the reaction, accompanied by the generation of Cu+. Thus, the quenched UCL intensity by Cu2+ could be recovered after adding GSH. However, after the reaction between ADA and GSH, the shoulder peak around 300 nm disappeared, which further turned out that the interaction between GSH and Cu2+ was interrupted because ADA could oxidize GSH to GSSG. Therefore, the recovered UCL intensity was quenched again due to the addition of ADA. These observations in UV–vis absorption spectra are similar to those for the sample without GQD-UCNPs (Fig. S10b). These results further confirm the effectiveness of our nanosensor for the detection of GSH and ADA.

To increase the sensitivity of this work, reaction conditions including the concentration of Cu2+, pH, temperature and reaction time were optimized. The UCL intensity recovery rate of the system is the highest when the concentration of Cu2+ ions was 60 μM, illustrating that the optimum reaction condition of Cu2+ concentration was 60 μM (Fig. 3 a). Besides, the effect of pH conditions on the detection results was investigated (Fig. 3b). The (I–I0)/I0 goes up as pH value increases from 3.0 to 4.5, whereas the opposite trend was observed when the pH exceeds 4.5. Thus, pH 4.5 was selected for subsequent studies. Results from investigating the optimum reaction temperature is exhibited in Fig. 3c. From the analysis, it was evident that the (I–I0)/I0 value reaches a peak at 37 °C. Therefore, the follow-up experiments were incubated at 37 °C. With regard to finding the optimal reaction time, the UCL intensity recovery rate was observed to reach a plateau within 15 min (Fig. 3d). Consequently, 15 min was chosen as the reaction time for the next studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and specificity of GSH and ADA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The UCL intensity of the GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ system had a gradual increase with the increasing of GSH concentration (Fig. 4 a). The UCL intensity recovery rate and the concentration of GSH (0.1 μM–2.0 μM) had an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.9981), with a linear equation of y = 0.7345x+0.05373 (Fig. 4b). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.04 μM based on the formula of LOD = 3δ/k (δ is the standard deviation of blank experiments, and K is the slope of the linear equation). Moreover, the specificity and anti-interference performances of the GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ system for GSH detection were conducted by adding relevant interfering substances. The UCL intensity was basically unchanged after adding 500 μM l-serine (L-Ser), l-tryptophan (L-Try), l-arginine (L-Arg), l-lysine (L-Lys), l-histidine (L-His), l-leucine (L-Leu), d-glutamic acid (D-Glu), l-aspartic acid (L-Asp), d-tyrosine (D-Tyr), l-cysteine (L-Cys), glucose, K+, Na+, Mg2+ and Ca2+ (Fig. 4c). Additionally, the UCL intensity increased significantly after treated with GSH (50 μM) in the interfering substances (Fig. 4d). These data suggested that the GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ system has excellent selectivity and anti-interference performances for GSH detection.

As the concentration of GSH increased, the UCL intensity of GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ system had a gradual recovery and achieved a plateau until GSH concentration increased to 50 μM, suggesting this to be the optimal concentration (Fig. 4b). In this vein, this concentration was selected for the further experiment. The UCL intensity of the GQD-UCNPs + Cu2+ +GSH system had a gradual decline with the addition of ADA from 0 μM to 20 μM (Fig. 5 a). When the [ADA] ranged from 0.1 μM to 1.0 μM, a good linear relationship between log [ADA] and the UCL intensity quenched rate ((I0–I)/I0) was observed (Fig. 5b). The linear equation was y = 0.2564x+0.7191 (x: log [ADA], y: (I0–I)/I0). The LOD was calculated to be 0.055 μM (LOD = 3δ/k), which was smaller than the maximal additive amount of ADA in flour (45 ppm)(L. Chen et al., 2016; Z. Chen et al., 2018). Furthermore, the performance of our assays for ADA detection was comparable to or even better than those published in other work (Table S1). Compared with traditional methods, such as HPLC, near-infrared spectroscopy technique, liquid chromatography electrospray tandem mass spectrometry and capillary zone electrophoresis, our method avoids the use of expensive instruments, tedious sample pre-treatment and the complex operating procedures. Compared with the colorimetric method, which has the advantage of simple and easy operation, our method has a certain improvement in the detection sensitivity. Fluorescence detection possesses excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, and easy operation. However, some of them with ultraviolet (UV)/visible light excitation have severe photobleaching and high background resulting in short duration of detection and high background interference. Our method selected UCNPs as luminescent nanomaterials with near-infrared excitation, which was beneficial to remove the interference of background luminescence, reduce photobleaching and improve the sensitivity of the luminescent probe. Overall, our method provides a sensing method for ADA with simple operation, high sensitivity and low background interference, which exhibits relatively satisfactory sensing capability compared to other detection methods.

To investigate the selectivity and anti-interference performances of our method for ADA detection, ADA and relevant interfering substances (L-Ser, L-Try, D-Asp, L-His, L-Arg, L-Lys, L-Leu, D-Glu, D-Tyr, L-Cys, glucose, K+, Na+, Mg2+, and Ca2+) were added into the solution. As expected, only the solution added with ADA (20 μM) showed a significant reduction in UCL intensity, whereas other systems with interfering substances (200 μM) presented no significant change in UCL intensity (Fig. 5c). Meanwhile, the UCL intensity was significantly quenched after adding ADA into the solution treated with interfering substances (Fig. 5d). The above results demonstrated the outstanding selectivity and anti-interference performances of the method, thus enhancing its potential prospects for ADA detection in real samples.

To further assess the reliability and practicability of the designed nanosensor, ADA analysis in flour samples was conducted. Market flour samples were pretreated using the method described in previous literature reports(Z. Chen et al., 2018). By spiking different concentrations of standard ADA (0 μM, 0.1 μM, 0.5 μM and 0.8 μM) into flour samples, the recovery experiment was conducted. No ADA was directly detected in flour samples (0.02 g/mL). The recovery rates of ADA in flour samples were between 91.3% and 104% (Table 1 ). All results indicated the outstanding practicality, feasibility and accuracy of the nanosensor for ADA detection in real samples.

In conclusion, we have designed a highly specific and sensitive nanosensor to detect ADA in flour based on upconversion luminescence with low fluorescence background and easy operation. The water solubility and UCL intensity of UCNPs were significantly enhanced by the introduce of GQD. Under the mediation of Cu2+, GQD-UCNPs were successfully applied for GSH and ADA detection. The interaction between GSH and Cu2+ and the ability of ADA to oxidize GSH are the keys to this strategy. The nanosensor exhibited high specificity and sensitivity for ADA detection with a LOD down to 0.055 μM. Furthermore, excellent recovery rates of ADA in spiked real samples were obtained, indicating that the designed method possesses good prospects for practical application. Notably, this method is the first application of UCNPs to detect ADA in flour, which broadens the application prospects of upconversion technology in the field of food safety.

Jialin Wang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yaqing Yang: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Aaron Albert Aryee: Writing – review & editing. Ningning Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhaohui Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (No. 232300421021 and No. 242300421342), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22004111 and No. 22274143), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2020M682327), Henan Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 202002009), the Tackle Key Problems in Science and Technology Project of Henan Province (No. 222102310386), Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Henan Province (No. 22TRTSTHN002).
